company.name: Lex Firm Global
company.description: Lex Firm Global operates at the intersection of law, power, and discretion. We do not advertise outcomes. We deliver them.
company.heroBody: Lex Firm Global serves a select group of clients who require the highest level of legal counsel and the strictest standards of confidentiality. We do not seek publicity. We seek results.

meta.title: Lex Firm Global | Elite Legal Counsel
meta.description: Lex Firm Global provides discreet, world-class legal counsel to high-net-worth individuals, corporations, and institutions across every jurisdiction.

about.heroSubtitle: Discretion is not a feature. It is the foundation.
about.storyTitle: Built on Silence. Proven by Results.
storyParagraphs: Lex Firm Global was founded in 1998 by a group of senior partners who shared a single conviction: that the most consequential legal work is never discussed in public.
Over the past 26 years we have advised heads of state, sovereign wealth funds, multinational corporations, and private individuals navigating matters of extraordinary complexity and sensitivity.
We do not publish client lists. We do not issue press releases about our victories. Our reputation is built entirely on the trust of those we serve and the results we achieve on their behalf.
Our practice spans every major jurisdiction. Our counsel is available around the clock. And our commitment to each client is absolute.

values.title: Absolute Discretion
values.desc: Every engagement is governed by the strictest confidentiality protocols. Nothing leaves our walls without your explicit consent.

values.title: Uncompromising Excellence
values.desc: We hold ourselves to a standard that most firms cannot reach. Every brief is treated as the most important matter we have ever handled.

values.title: Global Reach
values.desc: With counsel qualified across more than 40 jurisdictions, we operate wherever your matter requires, without limitation.

values.title: Decisive Action
values.desc: We do not deliberate when clarity is needed. We advise with precision and act with purpose.

highlights: Retained by clients in over 60 countries
Partner-led on every matter, without exception

services.title: International Arbitration
services.description: We represent clients in the world's most complex cross-border disputes before all major arbitral institutions.
services.fullDescription: Our international arbitration practice is one of the most active in Europe. We act as counsel in disputes before the ICC, LCIA, UNCITRAL, ICSID, and SIAC, across every sector and jurisdiction. Our team has handled disputes ranging from 50 million dollars to over 2 billion dollars, involving sovereign states, state-owned enterprises, and the world's largest corporations. We do not take cases we cannot win. And we prepare every case as if it will go the full distance.
details: ICC, LCIA, UNCITRAL, ICSID and SIAC proceedings
Enforcement of arbitral awards across jurisdictions
Appointment of arbitrators and challenge proceedings

services.title: Corporate Litigation
services.description: High-stakes commercial disputes handled with the precision and discretion that complex matters demand.
services.fullDescription: When a dispute threatens the existence or reputation of a business, the quality of legal counsel becomes the deciding factor. Our corporate litigation team has appeared in the Commercial Court, Court of Appeal, and Supreme Court of England and Wales, as well as in courts across the US, EU, and Asia. We handle shareholder disputes, breach of contract claims, director liability actions, and complex fraud litigation with equal facility.
details: Breach of contract and warranty claims

services.title: Asset Recovery
services.description: We locate, freeze, and recover misappropriated assets across jurisdictions with speed and legal force.
services.fullDescription: Asset recovery is one of the most technically demanding areas of law. It requires the ability to move faster than the opposing party, coordinate across multiple jurisdictions simultaneously, and apply legal tools that most practitioners have never used. Our team has recovered in excess of 3.2 billion dollars in assets for clients across 22 jurisdictions. We work with leading forensic accountants, intelligence firms, and local counsel to ensure that no asset remains beyond reach.
details: Worldwide freezing orders and Mareva injunctions
Cross-border asset tracing and forensic investigation
Enforcement of foreign judgments and awards
Proceeds of crime and confiscation proceedings

services.title: Private Wealth and Family Office
services.description: Discreet, comprehensive counsel for ultra-high-net-worth individuals, families, and their offices.
services.fullDescription: The legal needs of ultra-high-net-worth individuals and their families are unlike those of any other client group. They require counsel who understands not only the law but the broader context of wealth, family, and legacy. Our private wealth practice advises on the full spectrum of issues that arise in this space, from succession planning and trust structuring to family disputes and the protection of reputation.

services.title: Regulatory Defence
services.description: We defend individuals and institutions against regulatory investigations and enforcement actions worldwide.
services.fullDescription: Regulatory investigations can destroy careers, businesses, and reputations in a matter of weeks. Our regulatory defence team has represented senior executives, financial institutions, and public officials in proceedings before the SFO, FCA, DOJ, SEC, and equivalent bodies across Europe and Asia. We engage early, we engage aggressively, and we protect our clients at every stage of the process.
details: SFO, FCA, DOJ and SEC investigations
Dawn raid response and crisis management
Market abuse and insider trading defence

services.title: Investment Law
services.description: Strategic counsel for investors navigating complex regulatory environments and cross-border disputes.
services.fullDescription: Our investment law practice advises institutional investors, sovereign wealth funds, and private equity houses on the full lifecycle of cross-border investments. From structuring and regulatory approval through to dispute resolution and exit, we provide counsel that is both technically precise and commercially astute.

cases.title: Sovereign Asset Recovery: $680M Cross-Border Recovery
cases.summary: Acting for a sovereign government, we coordinated asset recovery proceedings across seven jurisdictions, securing freezing orders within 48 hours of instruction and ultimately recovering $680 million in misappropriated state funds.
cases.detail: This matter required simultaneous proceedings in England, Switzerland, the British Virgin Islands, the Cayman Islands, Singapore, the UAE, and the United States. We obtained worldwide freezing orders from the English Commercial Court within 48 hours of instruction, coordinated with local counsel in each jurisdiction, and worked alongside a leading forensic intelligence firm to trace assets that had been deliberately obscured through a network of shell companies and nominee arrangements. The matter concluded with a negotiated settlement that returned $680 million to the client state, together with a contribution to legal costs.

cases.title: ICC Arbitration: $420M Energy Sector Award
cases.summary: We represented a major energy company in ICC arbitration proceedings arising from the termination of a long-term supply agreement, securing a final award of $420 million in our client's favour.
cases.detail: The dispute arose from the wrongful termination of a 15-year liquefied natural gas supply agreement. The opposing party, a state-owned energy company, argued force majeure and regulatory impossibility. We successfully demonstrated that neither defence was available on the facts, and that the termination was commercially motivated. The tribunal awarded our client $420 million in damages, together with interest and costs. The award was subsequently enforced in three jurisdictions without challenge.

cases.title: Shareholder Derivative Defence: Tokyo and London
cases.summary: We successfully defended a major corporation and its board against coordinated shareholder derivative actions brought simultaneously in Tokyo and London, securing dismissal of both claims.
cases.detail: A group of activist shareholders brought coordinated derivative actions in the Tokyo District Court and the English Commercial Court, alleging breach of fiduciary duty by the board in connection with a major acquisition. We coordinated the defence across both jurisdictions, instructing leading local counsel in Tokyo while running the English proceedings ourselves. Both claims were dismissed at the preliminary stage. The English court awarded indemnity costs against the claimants.

cases.title: SEC Investigation: Favourable Resolution for Senior Executive
cases.summary: We represented a senior executive of a publicly listed company under investigation by the SEC for alleged market manipulation, securing a no-action outcome after 18 months of proceedings.
cases.detail: Our client, the Chief Investment Officer of a major asset management firm, was the subject of a formal SEC investigation into alleged market manipulation in connection with a series of large equity trades. We engaged with the SEC's enforcement division from the outset, managing the document production process, preparing our client for testimony, and presenting a comprehensive factual and legal response to the staff's preliminary findings. After 18 months, the SEC issued a no-action letter, closing the investigation without charges.

blog.title: The ESG Litigation Wave: What Boards Need to Know
blog.excerpt: Shareholder derivative actions targeting ESG discrepancies are accelerating. We examine the legal exposure and what boards can do to protect themselves.
blog.content:
The past 18 months have seen a significant increase in shareholder derivative actions targeting alleged discrepancies between corporate ESG commitments and actual practice. These claims are being brought in multiple jurisdictions simultaneously, and they are becoming more sophisticated in their legal theory and more aggressive in their remedies sought. Boards that have not yet conducted a rigorous audit of their ESG disclosures are exposed. The question is no longer whether ESG litigation will affect your company, but when.

In this analysis, we examine the key legal theories being deployed, the jurisdictions where exposure is greatest, and the steps that boards can take now to reduce their risk.

The primary legal theory in most ESG derivative actions is that directors breached their fiduciary duty by approving or failing to correct materially misleading ESG disclosures. In the United States, plaintiffs have had early success in Delaware courts by arguing that boards were aware of the gap between public commitments and internal practice and failed to act. In the United Kingdom, the Financial Conduct Authority has signalled that it will treat greenwashing as a disclosure failure with regulatory consequences, opening a parallel enforcement track alongside private litigation.

The jurisdictions of greatest current exposure are the United States, the Netherlands, Australia, and the United Kingdom. Each has seen at least one significant ESG-related derivative or class action reach the merits stage in the past 24 months. Germany and France are emerging as secondary fronts, driven by the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive and its mandatory disclosure requirements.

Boards that wish to reduce their exposure should begin with a gap analysis comparing public ESG commitments against internal data and operational reality. Where gaps exist, the board must decide whether to close them operationally or revise the public disclosure. Either path requires documentation of the decision-making process. Directors who can demonstrate that they were informed, deliberate, and acted in good faith are significantly better positioned to defend derivative claims than those who cannot.

blog.title: Post-LIBOR Transition: The Litigation Landscape
blog.excerpt: The transition away from LIBOR has created a new category of contractual dispute. We map the emerging litigation landscape and identify the key risk areas.
blog.content:
The cessation of LIBOR has given rise to a new and rapidly evolving category of contractual dispute. Parties to long-term financial contracts are discovering that the fallback provisions they agreed to years ago do not function as intended in a post-LIBOR world. The result is a growing body of litigation in which courts are being asked to interpret ambiguous contractual language, imply terms, and in some cases rectify agreements.

We have been instructed in a number of these matters and have developed a clear view of where the legal risk lies and how it can be managed.

The core problem is that many contracts written before 2014 contain fallback provisions that were designed to address a temporary unavailability of LIBOR, not its permanent cessation. When applied to a world in which LIBOR no longer exists, these provisions produce results that neither party intended. In some cases, the fallback rate is significantly higher or lower than the parties expected. In others, the fallback mechanism fails entirely, leaving the contract without a functioning interest rate provision.

English courts have so far taken a relatively conservative approach, declining to imply terms or rectify contracts where the existing language, however imperfect, produces a workable result. The US courts have been somewhat more willing to consider extrinsic evidence of the parties' intentions, though the outcomes remain unpredictable.

Parties with significant LIBOR-linked exposure who have not yet reviewed their contract portfolios should do so as a matter of urgency. The limitation period for claims arising from the transition is running, and early legal advice is significantly cheaper than late litigation.

blog.title: Cross-Border Enforcement: New Frontiers
blog.excerpt: Recent decisions in England, Singapore, and the UAE are reshaping the enforcement landscape for foreign judgments and arbitral awards.
blog.content:
The enforcement of foreign judgments and arbitral awards has always been one of the most technically demanding areas of international litigation. Recent decisions in England, Singapore, and the UAE have introduced new considerations that practitioners and their clients need to understand.

In England, the Supreme Court's decision in Privatbank v Kolomoisky has clarified the circumstances in which a foreign judgment obtained by fraud will be refused enforcement. The court confirmed that the fraud exception applies where the judgment was obtained by fraud on the foreign court, not merely where the underlying transaction was fraudulent. This is a narrower test than some practitioners had assumed, and it means that defendants seeking to resist enforcement on fraud grounds face a high evidential threshold.

In Singapore, the Court of Appeal has expanded the grounds on which enforcement of an arbitral award may be resisted on public policy grounds. The court held that an award that requires a party to perform an act that would be illegal under Singapore law may be refused enforcement even if the illegality was not raised before the tribunal. This decision has significant implications for awards involving regulated activities in Singapore, including financial services and real estate transactions.

In the UAE, recent legislative changes have significantly improved the prospects for enforcement of foreign awards in the DIFC courts. The DIFC Courts now have jurisdiction to enforce awards against assets located anywhere in the UAE, not merely within the DIFC itself. This change, combined with the UAE's accession to the New York Convention, makes the DIFC one of the most enforcement-friendly jurisdictions in the Middle East.

For clients with enforcement needs across these jurisdictions, the practical implications are significant. England remains the gold standard for enforcement of commercial awards, but the fraud exception is narrower than many assume. Singapore is becoming more cautious about awards with regulatory dimensions. And the UAE is rapidly becoming a viable enforcement destination for awards against Middle Eastern respondents.
